chunk_summary: Guidance on formula selection and safe weaning decisions for infants 0–24 months, including contraindications to breastfeeding, formula types for specific conditions, and early weaning considerations.
learning_objectives: Understand medical indications for formula feeding when breastfeeding is contraindicated or not feasible.
Identify appropriate formula types based on infant age and medical diagnosis.
Recognize risks of unmodified animal milk use before 12 months and counsel families on safe weaning practices.
key_facts: Classic galactosemia is an absolute contraindication to breastfeeding.
Standard cow’s milk-based formula is suitable for term infants up to 12 months if breastfeeding is not possible.
Extensively hydrolyzed or amino-acid formulas are recommended for confirmed cow’s milk protein allergy with significant symptoms.
Unmodified cow or buffalo milk should not be used as the main milk before 12 months due to high renal solute load and low iron content.
HIV-exposed infants in India are generally recommended exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis unless safe replacement feeding criteria are met.
reference_quote: WHO guidance generally favors exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis; replacement feeding is reserved for families who can reliably meet the criteria for safe and sustainable preparation. (Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Formula selection for specific medical conditions
